does: This survey provides a comprehensive framework and taxonomy for self-evolution in large language models—the paradigm where LLMs autonomously generate, refine, and learn from their own experiences without continuous external supervision. The authors propose a conceptual model decomposing self-evolution into four iterative phases: experience acquisition (how models generate their own training data), experience refinement (filtering and improving generated experiences), updating (incorporating refined experiences into the model), and evaluation (assessing progress). The survey categorizes different evolution objectives for both standalone LLMs and LLM-based agents, synthesizes existing literature across these dimensions, and identifies key technical challenges in each module of the self-evolution cycle.
connects: This work builds on long-standing research in machine learning's pursuit of reducing human supervision costs, connecting to prior work on self-training, bootstrapping, and unsupervised learning. It extends recent developments in instruction tuning and in-context learning by framing autonomous improvement as an iterative process inspired by human experiential learning. The survey draws on parallel lines of work in reinforcement learning from human feedback, chain-of-thought reasoning, and agent learning paradigms, while also connecting to earlier ideas about curriculum learning and self-supervised representation learning. It responds to recognized performance plateaus in supervised training of LLMs and scaling challenges that motivate more sample-efficient, autonomously-driven learning approaches.
enables: This framework enables several future research directions: developing more sophisticated experience acquisition methods that generate diverse and challenging tasks, improving refinement mechanisms to better identify high-quality self-generated data, designing updating strategies that efficiently integrate self-generated experiences without catastrophic forgetting, and creating robust evaluation protocols for autonomous progress. By systematizing self-evolution, the survey points toward pathways for scaling LLMs toward more general intelligence with reduced human annotation burden. It also opens questions about what constitutes valid self-supervision, how to detect and correct self-reinforcing errors, and whether autonomous learning can overcome task complexity ceilings—foundational challenges for achieving more capable autonomous agents.
